{ "runtimes": { "nvidia": { "args": [], "path": "nvidia-container-runtime" } }, "default-cgroupns-mode": "host" }

What is the size of the resulting relation for 1000 rows?
prediction: The resulting relation is six megabytes for 1000 rows: ``` rows | heap | total ------+--------+--------- 1000 | 512 kB | 6048 kB label | count ----------+------- Business | 250 Sci/Tech | 250 Sports | 250 World | 250 ``` Four megabytes of that is the vector column, stored in TOAST out-of-line because each vector exceeds Postgres's 2 KB in-line threshold. The 512 KB heap contains the `id`, `label`, `text`, and the pointers into TOAST. Sources: [pgvector-on-spark #4, pgvector-on-spark #2]

question: What was the instruction-following layer of the 49B model doing in the case of the question 'How did Tiger Woods finish the 2004 PGA Championship?'?
reference: err on the side of caution
prediction: The instruction-following layer of the 49B model was doing what it was trained to do, which was to err on the side of caution and refuse to commit to an answer when the top chunk didn't contain an explicit statement of the exact claim the user was asking for, in this case, the final round or tournament total. The 49B model saw the chunk that said "Tiger Woods … three-under-par 69" and chose not to commit, presumably because it didn't say "final round" or "tournament total" explicitly enough for its taste. The 8B model, on the other hand, committed to the answer because it was right. Sources: [bigger-generator-grounding-on-spark #2, bigger-generator-grounding-on-spark #2]
